# OPTICAL pH SENSOR FOR HYDROPONIC SYSTEMS

## Electronics Wiring, Component Layout & Timing Guide (Version 2.1)

This document provides the definitive wiring schematics, physical enclosure layout, and exact millisecond timing logic for the Version 2.1 Portable 3-Pump Architecture.

## 1\. COMPONENT PLACEMENT & ENCLOSURE LAYOUT

To build a portable, interference-free device, physical layout inside your 3D-printed or acrylic enclosure is just as important as the code.

### 1.1 The Rule of Isolation (Physical Zones)

Divide your enclosure into two distinct physical zones:

*   **The "Wet & High-Power" Zone:** Mount the three pumps, the 12V power distribution block, and the MOSFETs on one side.
*   **The "Dry & Logic" Zone:** Mount the ESP32, the TCS34725 sensor, and the flow cell on the opposite side.
*   _Why:_ Keeping 12V DC motor wiring away from the sensitive I2C sensor wires (SDA/SCL) prevents electromagnetic interference (EMI) from corrupting your colorimetric readings.

### 1.2 Flow Cell Mounting

*   Mount the 3D-printed main body so it is **tilted approximately 5 degrees** down towards the drain port (X=40).
*   Even though the R385 diaphragm pump acts as a wet-vacuum, giving gravity a slight assist ensures absolute zero pooling in the rectangular chamber corners.

### 1.3 Tubing Routing (3mm ID / 5mm OD Silicone)

*   Keep the tube length between the N20/NKP Dye Pump and the flow cell as **short as physically possible** (under 10cm). Shorter tubes reduce the amount of dye suspended in the line, preventing micro-drips between tests.

## 2\. MASTER WIRING SCHEMATIC & PINOUT

### 2.1 ESP32 DevKit Pin Assignments

| Component | ESP32 Pin | Wire Color | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| TCS34725 SDA | GPIO 21 | Green | I2C Data Line |
| TCS34725 SCL | GPIO 22 | Yellow | I2C Clock Line |
| Dual LEDs (Gate) | GPIO 2 | Red | Triggers the 2N2222 Transistor |
| Pump 1 (Water) | GPIO 4 | Orange | Triggers MOSFET 1 Gate |
| Pump 2 (Dye) | GPIO 5 | Purple | Triggers MOSFET 2 Gate (Supports PWM) |
| Pump 3 (Drain) | GPIO 18 | Blue | PWM Out (5 kHz, default 40% duty) |

### 2.2 The Power Distribution Network (Critical)

You cannot power 12V pumps from the ESP32. You must use a dual-rail power system.

1.  **Main Power Supply:** 12V DC, 2A (or higher) wall adapter.
2.  **12V Rail:** Directly powers the positive (+) terminals of all three pumps.
3.  **5V Rail:** Use a cheap **LM2596 Buck Converter** to step the 12V down to 5V. Wire this 5V to the VIN (or 5V) pin on the ESP32.
4.  **Common Ground:** Tie the grounds of the 12V supply, the ESP32, the TCS34725, and all MOSFETs together. _If grounds are not shared, the MOSFETs will not trigger._

### 2.3 Wiring the IRLZ44N MOSFETs (For the 3 Pumps)

For each of the three pumps, wire the IRLZ44N MOSFET exactly like this:

*   **Gate (Pin 1):** Connect to the designated ESP32 GPIO pin. _CRITICAL: Add a 10kΩ pull-down resistor between the Gate and Ground to prevent the pumps from turning on accidentally while the ESP32 boots up._
*   **Drain (Pin 2):** Connect to the Negative (-) wire of the pump motor.
*   **Source (Pin 3):** Connect directly to the System Ground.
*   **Flyback Diode:** You MUST solder a **1N4007 Diode** directly across the positive and negative terminals of every pump motor (Stripe facing the 12V side). _If you skip this, the magnetic field collapsing when the motor turns off will send a massive voltage spike backward and instantly destroy your MOSFETs and ESP32._

### 2.4 Wiring the Dual LEDs (2N2222 Transistor)

Because the 6500K and 3000K LEDs draw 40mA combined, wire them via an NPN Transistor (2N2222) to protect the ESP32:

*   **ESP32 GPIO 2** ![](data:image/png;base64,) 1kΩ Resistor ![](data:image/png;base64,) Transistor Base (Middle Pin).
*   **Transistor Emitter** ![](data:image/png;base64,) Connect to Ground.
*   **Transistor Collector** ![](data:image/png;base64,) Connect to the Negative legs of both LEDs.
*   **LED Positive Legs** ![](data:image/png;base64,) Connect to ESP32 3.3V pin (each LED needs its own 100Ω current-limiting resistor).

## 3\. PUMP TIMING CALCULATIONS (The Physics to Milliseconds)

To achieve the 17.09 mL operational capacity safely, the ESP32 translates volumetric fluid targets into exact millisecond delay() durations.

### 3.1 Pump 1 (Water Fill & Agitation)

*   **Hardware:** NKP-DC-S06B
*   **Rated Flow:** 37 mL / min at 100% Duty Cycle.
*   **Flow Rate per Second:** ![](data:image/png;base64,)

**State 1 (Base Fill Target: 14.80 mL)**

*   Time = ![](data:image/png;base64,)
*   **Firmware Timing:** digitalWrite(4, HIGH); delay(24000);

**State 4 (Agitation Blast Target: 1.10 mL)**

*   Time = ![](data:image/png;base64,)
*   **Firmware Timing:** digitalWrite(4, HIGH); delay(1780);

### 3.2 Pump 2 (Dye Micro-Dosing)

*   **Hardware:** NKP-DC-S06B
*   **PWM Setting:** Driven at 35% PWM (Duty Cycle 89/255).
*   **Geared Flow:** ~13 mL / min at 35% power.
*   **Flow Rate per Second:** ![](data:image/png;base64,)

**State 3 (Micro-Dose Target: 1.19 mL)**

*   Time = ![](data:image/png;base64,)
*   **Firmware Timing:** ledcWrite(pwmChannel, 89); delay(5500);

### 3.3 Pump 3 (Vacuum Drain)

*   **Hardware:** R385 Micro Diaphragm Pump
*   **Rated Flow:** 800 mL / min (or higher).
*   **PWM Setting:** 5 kHz PWM, default 40% duty. Duty can be adjusted at runtime (e.g., `/api/config?drainDuty=40`).
*   **Flow Rate per Second:** ![](data:image/png;base64,)

**State 7 (Total Chamber Evacuation: 17.09 mL)**

*   Time to drain fluid = ![](data:image/png;base64,).
*   To ensure the pump pulls the chamber completely dry and vacuums out any residual droplets from the corners, we apply a 2x safety multiplier.
*   **Firmware Timing:** ledcWrite(drainChannel, duty); delay(3000); ledcWrite(drainChannel, 0);
*   **Cool-Down Timing:** Pump remains off for 10,000 ms to allow motor cooling before the long idle period.

## 4\. TROUBLESHOOTING & CALIBRATION TIPS

1.  **Volume Verification:** Before running real chemicals, fill the dye reservoir and water reservoir with plain water. Run the test cycle. Place a graduated cylinder at the end of the R385 drain tube. After State 7, the cylinder should contain exactly 17.1 mL of water. If it is lower, your 12V power supply is likely sagging under load, and you may need to increase the delay() times slightly to compensate.
2.  **Initial PWM Calibration:** The 35% PWM speed (~13 mL/min) is an estimate. During initial setup, run Pump 2 into a tiny measuring cup for exactly 60 seconds at 35% PWM. If it dispenses 14 mL instead of 13 mL, adjust the ESP32 math to ensure the 1.19 mL dye dose is absolutely perfect.

_END OF DOCUMENT_
